title: loadNativeBinding swallows all candidate errors and attaches only the last one as `cause`
description: When both candidate paths fail the loop discards all but the last error and throws `CapabilityMissing` with only `lastError` as `cause`. If the prebuilt path fails with a meaningful diagnostic (e.g. wrong ABI, glibc mismatch) but the source path fails with a generic ENOENT, the useful error is silently dropped and only the generic ENOENT is surfaced to the caller. This makes diagnosing native-addon load failures in CI and production substantially harder.

title: resolvePrebuiltBinary accesses `process.platform` and `process.arch` with optional chaining but then passes a potentially-`undefined` string into `resolve()`
description: When `process` is unavailable (e.g. non-Node host), `process?.platform` evaluates to `undefined` and `process?.arch` evaluates to `undefined`, making the candidate path `<base>/undefined-undefined/jxl-native.node`. This is then passed to `require()` inside `loadNativeBinding`, which will throw ENOENT rather than a clear capability error. The fallback to `"unknown"` in the string template only applies if the property itself is absent, not if `process` is absent — `process?.platform` short-circuits to `undefined`, not `"unknown"`.

title: wrapDecoder.push() is not awaited and its result is not returned, so a rejected promise from the native push() is silently dropped
description: The `push` wrapper at line 382 calls `raw.push(chunk)` and returns `void` — it neither `await`s nor returns the result. The `NativeDecoder.push` interface declares the return type as `void | Promise<void>`. If the native binding returns a rejected `Promise<void>` (e.g. on a synchronous I/O error after an async yield), the rejection is swallowed and the caller receives `undefined`, with no error propagated. This can cause the encoder/decoder to silently stall after a partial push.

title: release() called in finally for close/cancel/dispose but not for push(), leaving inputDone unresolved if push throws synchronously before close is called
description: If `raw.push()` throws synchronously and the caller never calls `close()`, `cancel()`, or `dispose()`, the `inputDone` promise never resolves. Any consumer that calls `events()` will then `await inputDone` forever, producing a permanent hang. The risk is real when the native addon throws on malformed input during push without the caller having a teardown path (e.g. abrupt error in a streaming pipeline that skips close).

title: wrapDecoder double-wrap guard uses a duck-type property `__jxlWrappedEvents` that can be trivially defeated and does not protect against wrapping twice via different code paths
description: Both `wrapDecoder` (called from `adaptBindingCreators`) and `createNativeCodecFacade` call `wrapDecoder`. The guard `(raw as any).__jxlWrappedEvents` is checked before wrapping, but `adaptBindingCreators` sets up a `createDecoder` closure that wraps and then `createNativeCodecFacade.createDecoder` calls `wrapDecoder` again on the already-wrapped result. The guard prevents the second wrap correctly only if the first wrap set the flag — but because `adaptBindingCreators` wraps via its own closure that calls `wrapDecoder`, the returned decoder already has `__jxlWrappedEvents = true`, and when `createNativeCodecFacade` receives a pre-adapted binding (as in `loadNativeBinding` → `adaptBindingCreators` → `createNativeCodecFacade`) and wraps again, the guard fires. This is correct behavior — but the fragility of the sentinel means any future refactor that resets it will silently double-wrap. Consider asserting the flag is not already set rather than silently returning.
code_excerpt:
function wrapDecoder(raw: NativeDecoder): NativeDecoder {
  if ((raw as any).__jxlWrappedEvents) return raw;

title: normalizeEncoderOptions silently ignores duplicate `advancedFrameSettings` entries for the same setting id when merged from convenience fields
description: The check `!adv.some(x => x.id === id)` only skips adding the convenience field if the id already exists in the caller-supplied `advancedFrameSettings`. However it does not validate that the caller-supplied array itself contains duplicate ids. Duplicate ids in `advancedFrameSettings` are passed through to the native layer unfiltered, where behavior is libjxl-defined (last-write-wins, error, or silent ignore). No guard or deduplication is applied on the way in. Since `advancedFrameSettings` is an escape hatch, a developer error producing duplicate ids will produce silent incorrect behavior rather than a clear error.

title: guardDecoderOptions does not validate that the region fits within image dimensions, so an oversized region is silently passed to libjxl
description: The guard checks that `w > 0`, `h > 0`, `x >= 0`, `y >= 0` but does not validate that `x + w` or `y + h` fit within the image. The image dimensions are only known after the `header` event, so a pre-check here is impossible — but the current guard gives a false sense of safety. A caller passing `{ x: 0, y: 0, w: 999999, h: 999999 }` will pass the guard and reach libjxl which may clip silently or error with an opaque native error. Adding a comment noting this limitation (and what the caller should expect from the native layer) would prevent future confusion.

category: test: missing error event check / silent decode failure
title: maxPixels bomb-guard test uses `.rejects.toThrow()` on `decoder.close()` without awaiting it, so the assertion may pass vacuously if close() returns synchronously without rejecting
description: Line 496 calls `expect(decoder.close()).rejects.toThrow(...)` without `await`. If `decoder.close()` is synchronous (returns `void` not a `Promise`), `.rejects` receives a non-Promise and Bun/Jest will resolve it as a fulfilled value rather than a rejection, making the assertion pass even if the error is never thrown. The test appears to validate the decompression-bomb guard, but may be a false green. Should be `await expect(decoder.close()).rejects.toThrow(...)` or the close error should be checked inside the decoded events.

category: test: undeclared fields used without type guard
title: facade.test.ts passes `codestreamLevel`, `advancedControls`, and `ecResampling` fields that are not declared on `EncoderOptions` in index.ts, with no runtime or type-level guard in the production path
description: The tests at lines 62, 80-87, and 102-105 pass extra properties (`codestreamLevel`, `advancedControls: { buffering: { streamingInput: true } }`, `ecResampling: 4`) via type assertions (`as typeof encodeOptions & { ... }`). These fields are not present in `EncoderOptions` in `index.ts`. The tests expect `normalizeEncoderOptions` or the facade to lower these to `advancedFrameSettings` ids (34, 3), but no such lowering logic exists in the current `index.ts` `normalizeEncoderOptions`. Either the tests are testing functionality that was not yet implemented in the TS layer (only in native.cc), or the lowering logic has been omitted. Either way, the mismatch means the tests pass by virtue of the mock binding receiving whatever the facade forwards, without verifying the lowering actually happened via the TS layer.

title: Only one error class (CapabilityMissing) exists; native decode errors, encode errors, and image-too-large all surface as generic JS errors with no structured code field
description: The `{ type: 'error'; code: string; message: string }` decode event carries a `code` string from the native layer, but there is no TypeScript error class hierarchy for these codes (e.g. `DecodeError`, `EncodeError`, `ImageTooLargeError`). Callers must string-match on `.message` or the untyped `code` field to distinguish recoverable from fatal errors. Adding a small error taxonomy with typed `.code` values would make error handling at call sites explicit, catchable, and less brittle across libjxl version changes.
